attribution.note: Grammar architecture, historical and iconographic summaries

name: Visconti-Sforza Tarot — The Oldest Tarot (Bonifacio Bembo, c. 1451)
description:
The oldest tarot tradition we can hold in our hands. Around 1451 the workshop of Bonifacio Bembo painted a deck of 78 cards in gold leaf and tempera for the ducal house of Visconti-Sforza in Milan — not for fortune-telling, which did not yet exist, but for a trick-taking card game called 'trionfi' (triumphs), the ancestor of the word 'trumps'. This grammar holds the full historical deck: the 22 triumphs (major arcana) and the 56 minor cards across four suits — Coins, Cups, Swords, and Batons.

It is a historical and iconographic grammar, not a modern divination deck. Each card carries an Iconography note (what Bembo actually painted), a History note (its place, condition, and story), and a light Reading. Four cards never survived — The Devil, The Tower, the Knight of Coins, and the Three of Swords — and are included here as deliberate gaps. Six trumps were repainted by a later hand after the originals were lost. The famous Popess is almost certainly Sister Maifreda da Pirovano, a Visconti kinswoman burned for heresy in 1300.

Three related Bembo-workshop decks survive: the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone, c. 1442), the Brera-Brambilla (c. 1442, fragmentary), and this Pierpont Morgan-Bergamo deck (c. 1451), the most complete with 74 of 78 cards. Source images: Wikimedia Commons (public domain).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Bonifacio Bembo workshop, Pierpont Morgan-Bergamo Visconti-Sforza Tarot, c. 1451 (Pierpont Morgan Library, New York; Accademia Carrara, Bergamo; Colleoni collection) — gold-ground tempera on card, International Gothic style. Later restoration trumps attributed to Antonio Cicognara, c. 1480s–90s. Sister deck: Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot, c. 1442 (Beinecke Library, Yale) — the 'Damsel of Swords' and other six-court cards. All on Wikimedia Commons, Category:Visconti-Sforza tarot deck.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A ragged man in a tattered tunic, bare-legged, seven feathers standing in his hair and a club over his shoulder. He is the village madman, the social outcast painted with the same gold leaf as kings.
sections.History: Unnumbered and unnamed in the original game — the wild card that stands outside the sequence of triumphs. It survives in the Morgan Library portion of the deck.
sections.Reading: The zero before counting begins: the holy fool, innocence and folly walking together at the edge of the procession.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A young man seated at a table set with small objects, a wand or knife in hand — a market conjuror, cobbler, or sleight-of-hand trickster.
sections.History: "Bagatella" means a trifle; he is the lowest of the triumphs, the everyman who opens the parade. The earliest, most worldly figure of the trumps.
sections.Reading: Human skill and craft, the hand at work — beginnings rooted in the ordinary and the makeshift.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A crowned woman in the brown habit of a nun, a triple papal tiara on her head and a book in her lap.
sections.History: The most scandalous card in the deck: she is almost certainly Sister Maifreda da Pirovano, a Visconti kinswoman elected 'popess' by the heretical Guglielmite sect and burned at the stake in 1300. A buried family memory rendered in gold.
sections.Reading: Hidden wisdom and forbidden authority — the feminine that holds a sacred book the world would rather she did not open.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A crowned, enthroned woman bearing a shield emblazoned with the imperial eagle of the Visconti.
sections.History: Worldly feminine sovereignty wedded to the dynasty's heraldry — the Empress is the house made flesh.
sections.Reading: Abundance, fertile authority, the generative power that rules from a throne rather than a garden.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A bearded sovereign enthroned, the imperial eagle on his shield, an orb or sceptre in hand.
sections.History: Temporal masculine power — a portrait of dynastic legitimacy in an age when the Visconti and Sforza were buying and marrying their way toward a crown.
sections.Reading: Structure, command, the steadying weight of the throne and the law it enforces.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A pope in the triple tiara, hand raised in blessing, the crossed keys of Saint Peter near him.
sections.History: Spiritual authority answering the temporal Emperor — the sanctioned hierophant of the established Church.
sections.Reading: Tradition, blessing, the institutional channel through which the sacred is made official.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A couple stand hand in hand beneath a canopy while a blindfolded Cupid takes aim from a pedestal above them.
sections.History: Likely a Visconti-Sforza marriage portrait — possibly Francesco Sforza and Bianca Maria Visconti, whose union produced the deck's patrons. Here love is dynastic alliance.
sections.Reading: Union and choice, the binding of two houses — desire pressed into the service of lineage.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A crowned lady stands in a triumphal car, sometimes drawn by winged horses, holding orb and sceptre.
sections.History: The card that names the whole game: a Roman-style 'trionfo', the victory parade from which 'trumps' descend.
sections.Reading: Victory and momentum, the self carried forward in triumph over what it has mastered.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: An enthroned woman holds an upraised sword and a balance; above her a small armoured knight rides across the gold.
sections.History: In this pre-occult ordering Justice sits at VIII (the Golden Dawn would later swap it with Strength). The mounted knight is a Visconti flourish.
sections.Reading: Balance and reckoning, the blade and the scale — measure applied without flinching.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A stooped old man with a long beard holds aloft not a lantern but an hourglass, wrapped in heavy robes.
sections.History: Originally 'Il Tempo' — Time, or the Old Man — not yet the lamp-bearing Hermit of later decks. The hourglass marks his true subject.
sections.Reading: Time, age, and solitary measure — the slow accounting of hours that no triumph escapes.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

illustrations.scene: Wheel of Fortune (La Ruota della Fortuna)

sections.Iconography: A blindfolded Fortuna turns a great wheel; four figures cling to it, some sprouting asses' ears and tails as they rise and fall, mottoes streaming from their mouths.
sections.History: A medieval commonplace given Bembo's gold — 'regnabo, regno, regnavi, sum sine regno' (I shall reign, I reign, I have reigned, I am without reign).
sections.Reading: The turning of fortune, rise and fall — the one law that levels emperor and fool alike.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A standing figure subdues a lion, gripping it in a Hercules-like display of mastery.
sections.History: One of the six cards repainted by a later hand (c.1480s–90s, often attributed to Antonio Cicognara) after the Bembo original was lost. The style is visibly different from the first artist's.
sections.Reading: Fortitude — courage that masters the beast not by force alone but by steady will.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A youth hangs upside down by one ankle from a wooden frame, hands bound behind his back, the free leg crossed.
sections.History: A 'pittura infamante' — the shaming portrait Italian cities painted of traitors and debtors, hung by the foot. Only later did occultists read it as sacrifice.
sections.Reading: Suspension and reversal — the inverted view, the betrayer's punishment, the world seen from upside down.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A gaunt skeletal figure stands on foot drawing a great bow, or bearing a scythe across barren ground.
sections.History: Unnamed and unnumbered in the original deck — the card too plain to need a label. The great equaliser of the danse macabre.
sections.Reading: Transition and the great levelling — the end that makes no exception for rank or gold.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A winged woman pours liquid between two vessels in an unbroken stream.
sections.History: Another of the six later-hand restorations. Temperance's gesture — mixing without spilling — is among the oldest stable tarot images.
sections.Reading: Tempering and flow — the patient blending of waters, moderation as a living art.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: No Bembo card survives. Later decks in the tradition show a horned, clawed figure presiding over bound captives; the Visconti-Sforza original is simply gone.
sections.History: One of four cards never recovered from this deck. Whether it was lost, destroyed, or — as some suggest — deliberately removed, we cannot know. Its absence is part of the deck's history.
sections.Reading: Bondage, appetite, the shadow side of power — read here through a deliberate, instructive silence.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: No Bembo card survives. In the broader tradition the Tower — 'the House of the Devil', or 'the Fire' — shows a struck tower and falling figures.
sections.History: The second of the deck's permanently lost cards. The early Italian decks often called it the Fire or the House of God; here only the gap remains.
sections.Reading: Sudden collapse and the breaking of false structures — present in this deck only as a missing card, an emptiness where catastrophe should be.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A woman stands holding aloft a single great eight-pointed star against the gold.
sections.History: One of the six later restorations. The Visconti Star is a simple votive figure, far from the kneeling water-pourer of the Marseille and Waite decks.
sections.Reading: Hope and orientation — a fixed light to steer by after the dark of Death and the lost Tower.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A woman holds up a crescent moon, robed and serene.
sections.History: Later-hand restoration. The eerie dogs-and-crayfish landscape of later Moon cards is a much later invention; here the Moon is simply held, like an attribute.
sections.Reading: Reflection and the night mind — the borrowed light that governs tides and dreams.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: A winged child (putto) floats above a hilly landscape, lifting a radiant human-faced sun overhead.
sections.History: Among the six repainted cards. The cherub bearing the sun is one of Bembo's most charming surviving compositions in the restored hand.
sections.Reading: Vitality and the lit world — radiance carried by something small and unafraid.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: God the Father with attendant angels sounds the trumpet above; below, the dead rise naked from their tombs.
sections.History: Later-hand restoration. A straightforward Last Judgement, the most explicitly Christian of the trumps.
sections.Reading: The call and the reckoning — awakening, summons, the dead rising to answer.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Pierpont Morgan Library, New York / Accademia Carrara, Bergamo

sections.Iconography: Two winged putti hold up a great orb in which a walled city floats above water and hills, crowned by a star or a standing figure.
sections.History: The last and highest triumph, and the final card of the six restorations — the triumph that overcomes all other triumphs.
sections.Reading: Completion and wholeness — the cosmos held up to be seen, the journey of the trionfi closed.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Ace of Coins: a single coin arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.
sections.History: A pip card of Coins (Denari), the suit of Earth. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Ace of Coins — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Two of Coins: 2 coins arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.

sections.Reading: Two of Coins — duality and exchange — balance, pairing, the first relationship, within the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.

sections.Iconography: Page of Coins: a courtly fante figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the coin of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Coins (Denari). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: Page of Coins — the student and messenger of the suit — its energy young, curious, not yet committed, expressed through the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

illustrations.edition: Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) Tarot, c. 1442 (sister deck)

sections.Iconography: Knight of Coins: a courtly cavallo figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the coin of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: The Knight of Coins is one of the four cards lost from the Morgan-Bergamo deck — no Morgan-Bergamo example survives. The image shown here is the Knight of Coins from the Cary-Yale sister deck (c. 1442, same Bembo workshop), where it does survive, standing in for the lost original.
sections.Reading: Knight of Coins — the suit in motion — questing, active, riding out to meet its element, expressed through the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Queen of Coins: a courtly regina figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the coin of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Coins (Denari). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: Queen of Coins — the inward mastery of the suit — it held, nurtured, and understood from within, expressed through the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: King of Coins: a courtly re figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the coin of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Coins (Denari). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: King of Coins — the outward authority of the suit — it commanded, ordered, and ruled in the world, expressed through the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Ace of Cups: a single cup arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.
sections.History: A pip card of Cups (Coppe), the suit of Water. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Ace of Cups — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Two of Cups: 2 cups arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.
sections.History: A pip card of Cups (Coppe), the suit of Water. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Two of Cups — duality and exchange — balance, pairing, the first relationship, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Three of Cups — first increase — growth, fellowship, the suit beginning to flower, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Four of Cups: 4 cups arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.

sections.Reading: Four of Cups — stability and foundation — a settled square, rest, possession, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Five of Cups — tension and loss — friction, lack, the challenge within the suit, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Six of Cups — harmony and reciprocity — give and take in balance, ease restored, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Seven of Cups: 7 cups arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.

sections.Reading: Seven of Cups — striving — effort against resistance, partial and uncertain gain, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Eight of Cups — movement and change — energy in motion, swiftness, reordering, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Nine of Cups — near-fullness — intensity at its height, the suit almost complete, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Ten of Cups: 10 cups arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.

sections.Reading: Ten of Cups — completion — the suit in full, its plenty and its burden together, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Page of Cups: a courtly fante figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the cup of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Cups (Coppe). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: Page of Cups — the student and messenger of the suit — its energy young, curious, not yet committed, expressed through feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Knight of Cups: a courtly cavallo figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the cup of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Cups (Coppe). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: Knight of Cups — the suit in motion — questing, active, riding out to meet its element, expressed through feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

illustrations.edition: Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) Tarot, c. 1442 (sister deck)

sections.Iconography: King of Cups: a courtly re figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the cup of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Cups (Coppe). The Morgan-Bergamo King of Cups survives but has not been digitized on Wikimedia Commons, so the image shown here is the King of Cups from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) deck — the same Bembo workshop, about a decade earlier. In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King; the Cary-Yale deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: King of Cups — the outward authority of the suit — it commanded, ordered, and ruled in the world, expressed through feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Ace of Swords: a single sword arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.
sections.History: A pip card of Swords (Spade), the suit of Air. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Ace of Swords — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within mind and conflict — thought, strife, decision, and the cut of judgment.

sections.Iconography: Three of Swords: 3 swords arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.
sections.History: The Three of Swords is one of the four cards lost from the Morgan-Bergamo deck. The image shown here is the Three of Swords (Spade) from the Cary-Yale sister deck (c. 1442, same Bembo workshop), where it survives, standing in for the lost original.
sections.Reading: Three of Swords — first increase — growth, fellowship, the suit beginning to flower, within mind and conflict — thought, strife, decision, and the cut of judgment.

sections.Iconography: Ace of Batons: a single baton arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.

sections.Reading: Ace of Batons — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.

sections.History: A pip card of Batons (Bastoni), the suit of Fire. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Four of Batons — stability and foundation — a settled square, rest, possession, within will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Five of Batons: 5 batons arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.
sections.History: A pip card of Batons (Bastoni), the suit of Fire. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Five of Batons — tension and loss — friction, lack, the challenge within the suit, within will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Ten of Batons — completion — the suit in full, its plenty and its burden together, within will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Page of Batons: a courtly fante figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the baton of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Batons (Bastoni). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: Page of Batons — the student and messenger of the suit — its energy young, curious, not yet committed, expressed through will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Knight of Batons: a courtly cavallo figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the baton of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.

sections.Reading: Knight of Batons — the suit in motion — questing, active, riding out to meet its element, expressed through will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Queen of Batons: a courtly regina figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the baton of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.

sections.Reading: Queen of Batons — the inward mastery of the suit — it held, nurtured, and understood from within, expressed through will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: King of Batons: a courtly re figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the baton of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.History: A court card of Batons (Bastoni). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: King of Batons — the outward authority of the suit — it commanded, ordered, and ruled in the world, expressed through will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.About the Triumphs: The 22 trumps are the tarot's true invention — a fifth suit of allegorical 'triumphs' added to an ordinary 56-card pack around 1440. They were not yet a spiritual journey; they were the high cards of a trick-taking game, a parade of estates from Fool to World. Only in the 18th century did occultists reread them as the soul's progress.
sections.How to Use: Browse the trumps in order to see the medieval social cosmos the deck assumes: craftsman, popess, the imperial and papal powers, love, fortune, the virtues, death, the heavens, and the world entire.

sections.About this Suit: Coins (Denari) is the suit of Earth — money, body, land, and provision. In the Morgan-Bergamo deck its pips are gilded discs amid scrolling vines; the Knight of Coins is one of the deck's four lost cards.
sections.How to Use: The suit of having and making — read it for questions of resources, work made tangible, and the slow accumulation of the material world.

sections.About this Suit: Cups (Coppe) is the suit of Water — feeling, love, and devotion. Its emblem is the chalice, and in a deck made to celebrate a marriage it carries the deck's romantic charge.
sections.How to Use: The suit of the heart — read it for relationship, longing, and the inner life that the trumps dramatise on a cosmic scale.

sections.About this Suit: Swords (Spade) is the suit of Air — mind, conflict, and decision. Its curved blades interlace across the gold; the Three of Swords is one of the deck's four lost cards.
sections.How to Use: The suit of the cutting mind — read it for thought, strife, and the hard clarity of judgment.

sections.About this Suit: Batons (Bastoni), or Staves, is the suit of Fire — will, work, and growth. It is the deck's only complete minor suit, all fourteen cards surviving.
sections.How to Use: The suit of action and enterprise — read it for ambition, labour, and the green energy of beginnings pushed into the world.

sections.About the Lost Cards: Four cards of this deck never survived: The Devil, The Tower, the Knight of Coins, and the Three of Swords. Whether lost to time, accident, or — in the case of the two dark trumps — possibly deliberate removal, we cannot know. Their absence is itself a historical document.
sections.Why this Matters: The two missing trumps are precisely the deck's images of bondage and catastrophe. A deck made to glorify a dynasty has come down to us with its pictures of the Devil and the falling Tower torn out — a gap that later traditions had to imagine their way across.

items.name: The Restoration Hand — Six Repainted Triumphs

sections.About these Cards: Six trumps — Fortitude, Temperance, the Star, Moon, Sun, and World — were repainted by a later artist (c. 1480s–90s, often attributed to Antonio Cicognara) after the Bembo originals were lost. Their style, faces, and gold differ visibly from the first hand.
sections.Why this Matters: The deck we call 'Visconti-Sforza' is already a restoration — a 15th-century object repaired within its own century. The seams between the two hands are a lesson in how every old deck is partly a reconstruction.
